Education and Well-being
U
N
I

1
The first unit of this book explores the concept of physical 
education as a holistic approach to nurture the body, mind, and 
spirit. It emphasises the importance of physical activities, games, 
yoga, and sports in promoting physical fitness, emotional balance, 
mental calmness, and social values like respect, fairness, and 
cooperation.
The unit highlights the National Curriculum Framework for 
School Education (NCF-SE 2023), which integrates traditional 
Indian practices with modern sports to foster overall wellness 
and personal growth. The unit explores the meaning, definitions, 
aims, and objectives of physical education. It goes beyond physical 
activity, highlighting learning through the body to develop 
teamwork, discipline, and self-control. Scholars like Sri Aurobindo 
and organisations, like UNESCO, have highlighted the role of 
physical education in achieving holistic development. The aims 
include building character, instilling virtues such as, honesty 
and patience, and preparing students to be socially responsible 
and emotionally balanced individuals. This unit also focuses on 
physical literacy which is defined as the ability, confidence, and 
motivation to engage in physical activities throughout life. It 
includes developing locomotor, non-locomotor, and manipulative 
skills, which are essential for fitness, holistic health and overall 
development. The unit encourages students to reflect on their 
experiences with physical education and its impact on their growth. 
It also promotes the integration of fitness and well-being into daily 
life while informing students about various career pathways in 
physical education.


The word ‘education’ creates an initial perception of books, classrooms, and 
exams. However, real education is not only about the mind, it is about the 
body as well. If the body is weak or unhealthy, the mind cannot focus, and if 
the mind is unwell, even a strong body feels tired.
	
In the Preparatory and Middle stages of school, you were introduced to 
various indigenous games, traditional sports, āsanas, prāṇāyāma, and other 
yogic practices. These activities not only built physical strength and stamina 
but also provided mental peace, discipline, and balance.
	
That is why physical education is an important part of our lives. It 
teaches us how to take care of our health, move and play, stay active, and 
balance studies with fitness. The National Curriculum Framework for 
School Education (NCF-SE 2023) names the subject as Physical Education and 
Well-being, since it not only helps us become physically fit and active but also 
promotes happiness, confidence, emotional balance, and mental calmness. 
It nurtures important values, such as, respect for others, fairness, honesty, 
and co-operation.

3
Physical Education and Well-being
Before we explore the meaning, aims, and importance of Physical 
Education, let us ask ourselves — how does Physical Education shape 
our bodies and improve the quality of our lives? How are movement, 
discipline, and fitness closely linked to living a better life?
Meaning and Definitions of Physical Education
Physical Education is not just restricted to a sports period or an 
exercise class in school. It is not limited to drills, games, or physical 
activity alone, but is rather a process of learning through the body. It 
trains the body and the mind together. Every sprint develops speed 
and each repeated sprint improves endurance; every yoga posture 
builds balance and self-control; and every game teaches teamwork, 
strategy, confidence, and discipline — lessons that are useful in 
everyday life.
	
“The perfection of the body, as great a perfection as we can 
bring about by the means at our disposal, must be the ultimate 
aim of physical culture. Perfection is the true aim of all culture, the 
spiritual and psychic, the mental, the vital and it must be the aim of 
our physical culture also.” – Sri Aurobindo
	
“Physical education is the process of education through physical 
activities. It is the development of the total personality of the child 
to its fullness and perfection in body, mind, and spirit.” — Central 
Advisory Board of Physical Education and Recreation (1959, India)
Fig. 1.1


4
Khel Praveen
	
“Physical education is education through physical activities for 
the development of the total overall personality of the child and its 
fulfilment and perfection in body, mind, and spirit.” — J.P. Thomas 
(1963 Indian educationist)
	
“A fundamental right for all, physical education is an essential 
element of lifelong learning that contributes to holistic development, 
health, and quality of life.” — UNESCO (1978) International Charter 
of Physical Education and Sport, Paris
	
“Physical education is an integral part of the total education 
process. Its aim is the development of physically, mentally, 
emotionally, and socially fit citizens through the medium of physical 
activities.” — Charles A. Bucher (Bucher, C. A. 1979)
	
“Physical 
Education 
consists 
of

sports, and other activities that promote 
mind-body wellness. Physical Education 
should provide a wide range of age-

activities that develop knowledge of the body, 
and of games and sports, together with a 
disposition towards perseverance, teamwork, 
and sportsmanship.” – NCF-SE 2023
Aims of Physical Education
Imagine your school team is preparing for an inter-house sports 
event. Students of different backgrounds, abilities, and personalities 
are grouped together and asked to plan their 
practice sessions. Some of your teammates 
are fast runners, while some struggle with 
stamina. During practice, disagreements 
might arise. At times, a team loses a match, 
and at other times, they win.
	
Physical Education is not limited to your 
physical training alone. It develops character 
and inculcates virtues such as honesty, 
patience, and fairness. When students play 
together, they learn to respect rules, accept 
victory with humility, and face defeat with

Objectives of Physical Education
The objectives of physical education encompass the various ways in 
which it supports the growth of students and prepares them for the 
future.

Policy Support: The NEP 2020 mandates Physical Education and

Physical Literacy
How did you learn movements like throwing a 
ball, running, or balancing on one leg? Did you 
learn everything at once? What helped you to 
finally do them correctly?
As you have learnt in your previous grades, it 
is physical literacy that enables us to execute 
various physical activities with ease in our daily 
life. Just as reading and writing develop language 
literacy, learning to move confidently while 
performing physical activities develops physical 
literacy. It means having the ability, confidence, 
and motivation to participate in different physical 
activities throughout life.
READ AND REFLECT
Richa is a Grade 9 student, who spends most of her free time on mobile 
games  and rarely engages in outdoor activities. Recently, her teachers 
noticed that she often feels tired, struggles to concentrate in class, and 
gets stressed before exams. During the annual sports day, her friends 
encouraged her to join a team game. At first, she hesitated, but slowly 
she started enjoying it. Over time, as she participated more in sports, she 
became more energetic, her focus in studies improved, and she learned 
how to co-operate with teammates. Her teachers also observed that she 
became calmer and more confident, even when she made mistakes.
•	
Which two objectives of physical education helped Richa improve her 
concentration and confidence?
•	
How did participation in team games help Richa in her social 
development?
•	
If Richa continues to play regularly and maintains healthy habits, 
which long-term benefit of physical education will she achieve?
Fig. 1.4


7
Physical Education and Well-being
Physical Literacy includes:
•	
Physical ability such as running, throwing, balancing,
and jumping
•	
Confidence in one’s own movement and skills
•	
Motivation and interest to stay active and
•	
Understanding why physical activity is important for our 
health and well-being
Benefits of Physical Literacy
After school, you might find yourself sitting for long hours — 
scrolling through your phone, watching videos, or playing games 
with little or no movement. Gradually, over time, the body feels stiff, 
muscles grow weaker, and the mind becomes tired or stressed. When 
you choose to step outside, play a sport, practise a skill, or simply 
move more, you feel a difference in your body and energy levels.
	
Developing physical literacy helps students build fitness, 
confidence, and healthy habits throughout their life. Several
benefits of physical literacy are listed below:
•	
Improves overall health and physical fitness
•	
Builds confidence and self-esteem
•	
Helps prevent lifestyle diseases
•	
Develops teamwork, leadership, and decision-making skills
•	
Reduces anxiety and improves focus in studies
Movement skills are the building blocks of all 
physical activities. These fall under the umbrella 
of physical literacy and can be categorised into 
the following:
Locomotor Skills
You have learnt skills like walking, jumping, 
leaping, 
hopping, 
and 
skipping 
in 
the 
Preparatory and Middle stages. These skills 
develop strength, speed, agility, and endurance 
which are known as locomotor skills. Activities 
like Kabaddi and Kho-Kho depend heavily on 
Fig. 1.5


8
Khel Praveen
Fig. 1.7
Fig. 1.6
these skills for chasing, dodging, and changing 
direction quickly.
Non-locomotor Skills
Non-locomotor skills are movements performed 
without mobilising the body from one place 
to another. These include bending, stretching, 
twisting, turning, balancing, and swaying. They 
develop stability, flexibility, and body control. 
Yoga predominantly uses non-locomotor skills. In 
postures like Tāḍāsana or Vṛikṣhāsana, learners 
must stay balanced and focused without moving 
from their positions. 
Manipulative Skills
Manipulative skills involve controlling objects with hands or feet. 
These include squeezing, throwing, catching, kicking, striking, 
rolling, and dribbling. They build hand-eye coordination, timing, 
focus, and judgement. Games like Gillī-Daṇḍā, cricket, football, 
and volleyball depend on manipulative skills. They help link brain 
coordination with physical action.
	
Movement skills are at the heart of physical 
literacy games like Āṭyā-Pāṭyā, Anding Oka, 
Turai Kar, Nāḷugu Rāḷḷu  Āṭa, Ghorpaḍīchhe 
Śhepūta, etc., helping students develop speed, 
agility and coordination, while yoga asanas 
improve balance, stability, and body awareness. 
These activities reflect India’s cultural heritage 
and support holistic development.
Explore 
your 
school’s 
library, 
search 
for Khel Yoga and Khel Yatra books of 
previous grades, and list down locomotor, 
non-locomotor and manipulative activities 
mentioned in them. Try to perform any 
two activities.

On a clear morning, in an ancient gurukula, a group of young students wake 
up before sunrise. After prayers, they begin their day with archery practise, 
aiming arrows at wooden targets set under the trees. Some students wrestle 
in the mud courtyard, while others engage in farm work or practise yoga 
postures. Only after this physical training, do they gather around their teacher 
for lessons in the Vedas, grammar, and philosophy.
	
From the gurukulas of the Vedic age to universities of ancient India, 
physical education had been practised regularly. This chapter takes you on 
a journey through tradition, showing how physical education was practised, 
celebrated, and gradually woven into daily life. We will also replicate these 
practices according to the contemporary scenario.
The concept of education in India has always been holistic. 
It was never limited to reading and writing. Students were 
trained to develop the body and the mind together. Hence, 
physical training exercises, games, martial arts, and yoga 
were an important part of ancient life. Evidences for these 
are also found in the Vedas, epics, Ayurveda, and even 
various archaeological findings.

Physical Education in India

done only up to half of one’s capacity.

From Independent India to Present


13
History and Culture of Physical Education in India
Activity
Long before marked playgrounds and stadiums existed, physical 
activity was part of everyday life. In early Indian societies, physical 
activities referred to daily movements, such as walking, running, 
swimming, lifting, farming, hunting, bathing, and martial training. 
Games were not separate leisure activities but were connected with 
health, discipline, social interaction, and preparation for life. For 
example, children learned to run while helping elders, balance while 
walking on uneven paths, and work together.
	
Cleanliness, strength, discipline, and co-operation were essential 
for survival and community living. Over time, these daily movements 
developed into organised training, martial skills, wrestling traditions, 
and, later, structured games. Physical fitness was valued as a means of 
maintaining health, fulfilling duties, and strengthening community 
life. Let us explore how physical activities and games evolved in 
early Indian society from prehistoric times to the colonial period.

The rock paintings of pre-historic times show that early humans 
engaged in various activities that built physical strength, endurance, 
coordination, and cooperation. These activities required strength, 
endurance, and teamwork. The UNESCO World Heritage Report on 
the Rock Shelters of Bhimbetka records paintings depicting hunting

scenes, use of weapons, and collective activities indicating that 
physical movement was central to daily life and social organisation.
	
People of the Sindhu-Sarasvatī civilisation led active daily lives. 
Physical activity was mainly linked to agriculture, construction, 
craftwork, walking through planned cities, and managing water 
systems. Structures such as the Great Bath of Mohenjo-Daro and 
water reservoirs at Dholavira show the importance of cleanliness, 
movement, and community effort. Physical activity was a part of 
daily work and community living.
The Ṛigveda entails warriors, chariots, 
weapons, and physical strength indicating 
that courage, endurance, and combat 
ability were highly valued in early society.

from

It

physical

the

healthy life.

History and Culture of Physical Education in India
BEFORE WE MOVE ON
Vasiṣhṭha’s Dhanurveda-saṁhitā, belonging to the medieval period 
describes systematic training in archery, including posture, balance, 
controlled movement, and discipline. It also emphasises moderation 
in physical effort and ethical conduct, showing that martial training is 
focused on strength, control, and responsibility, rather than competition 
or sport.
	
During the Epic period, physical 
training became more organised 
and purposeful, especially as part of 
education and preparation for  life. 
The great Indian epics, the Rāmāyaṇa 
and the Mahābhārata, describe how 
students were trained in archery, the 
use of weapons, physical strength 
and discipline under the guidance 
of teachers (Guru) in gurukulas. 
The Mahābhārata provides detailed 
descriptions of systematic training where students practised 
archery, mace fighting, and other forms of warfare including horse 
and chariot riding. These epics show that physical activity during 
the Epic period was closely linked with education, physical training, 
discipline, courage, and moral values.
शरीरचेष्ा या चेष्ा स्थैर्यार्था बलवर्धिनी ।
देहव्यायामसङ्ख्याता मात्रया तां समाचरेत् ॥
	
	
	
– चरकसंहिता, सूत्रस्ान 7, 31
Śharīra-cheṣhṭā yā cheṣhṭā sthairyārthā balavardhinī ।
Deha-vyāyāma-saṅkhyātā mātrayā tāṁ samācharet ॥
– Charaka-saṁhitā, Sūtra-sthāna, 7, 31
The action of the body meant for stability and for increasing 
strength is known as physical exercise (Deha-vyāyāma). Such 
suitable physical movement bringing stability (in body) and 
increase in strength is known as vyāyāma (physical exercise). 
This has to be practiced in moderation (with apt control 
and for proper duration).

DID YOU KNOW?
In South India, kalaripayaṭṭu emerged as a systematic martial art practised 
in spaces for kalari that focused on flexibility, balance, strength, agility, 
and weapon training.
	
Wrestling (malla-yuddha) was practised in akhāḍās in northern, 
central, and western regions of India, while various stick and sword 
practices developed in eastern and central India. Although these traditions 
have ancient roots, most written records describing their methods belong 
to the medieval period.
	
During the early classical period, ideas about physical activity 
became more systematic, especially in relation to health, discipline, 
and state responsibilities. Texts from this period show that physical 
strength and bodily control were considered important for both 
personal well-being and effective administration. The Arthaśhāstra 
by Kauṭilya describes physical discipline, regular routine, and 
fitness particularly for rulers, soldiers and officials. The text also 
highlights cleanliness, order, and regulation of daily activities in 
cities, showing that physical preparedness and public health were 
matters of state concern.
	
According to ancient Indian knowledge, exercise is any physical 
activity of the body that is beneficial, improves stability, and 
increases strength. Such activity is called vyāyāma. However, this 
exercise should always be done in 
moderation, not excessively.
	
According to Āchārya Charaka, 
regular physical exercise makes 
the body light, strong, stable, and 
hardworking. It also improves pain 
tolerance, helps in balancing 
doṣhas and strengthens 
digestion and metabolism. 
All these benefits are 
achieved through 
proper exercise.
Fig. 2.4

History and Culture of Physical Education in India
Medieval Physical Culture
The Malla-purāṇa (13th century CE) describes 
16 types of wrestling exercises for strength and 
stamina. Wrestlers were expected to practice 
daily, combining drills with yoga postures. 
Wrestling was both a sport and a way to build 
character. The Mānasollāsa (12th century CE), 
written by King Someśhvara III, is the first text 
to mention Mallakhamb as a unique Indian 
sport where athletes perform acrobatic poses 
on a wooden pole. This exercise improved 
strength, balance, and flexibility, and its royal 
patronage 
shows 
how 
highly it was valued.
	
In medieval times, wrestling (malla-yuddha) 
became 
a 
community 
sport. 
Towns 
had 
akhāḍās 
(wrestling 
schools) 
where 
young people trained in daṇḍs 
(push-ups), baiṭhaks (squats), and 
weight lifting. Wrestling matches 
were held at fairs and festivals, 
attracting 
large 
crowds 
and 
strengthening community bonds.
Late Medieval Period
During the late medieval period, physical activities were closely 
connected with military preparation and administration. The 
Āʾīn-i-Akbarī records that wrestling, archery, horse riding, and 
hunting were encouraged during the reign of Akbar. Contemporary 
Marathi chronicles describing the life of Chhatrapati Shivaji 
Maharaj, and Rajputana history describing the life of western Indian 
warriors show that physical endurance, agility, weapon training, 
and movement across difficult terrain were essential for warfare 
and defence. These activities focused on physical strength, mental 
resilience and readiness, unlike organised games.

Fig. 2.6

Global Origins of Physical Education
While India followed its own traditions, in Europe the modern 
subject of Physical Education began to take shape in the 18th and 
19th centuries. The term itself first appeared in English in 1748, in 
a translation of a French book, where it spoke about ‘the physical 
education of children’. This shows that by the mid-1700s, Europeans 
had begun to think of training the body as an important part of 
education.
	
By the late 1700s, educational reformers were treating physical 
training as a part of general schooling. In Germany, Johann Christoph 
Friedrich GutsMuths, often called the “grandfather of Gymnastics”, 
published Gymnastics for Youth in 1793. He designed exercises and 
games for children as part of their overall development. Another 
German, Friedrich Ludwig Jahn, wrote Die deutsche Turnkunst in 
1816, promoting Turnen or organised gymnastics to build discipline, 
strength, and national pride. In Sweden, Per Henrik Ling developed 
a system of gymnastics that combined medical, military, and 
educational training.
	
These ideas also spread to America. In 1825, Charles Beck, a 
German who had moved to the United States, introduced gymnastics 
at the Round Hill School in Massachusetts and set up the first school 
gymnasium in the country. A few years 
later, in 1828, he translated Jahn’s book into 
English as  ‘Treatise on Gymnasticks’. In 
the introduction, Beck even used the term 
physical education, helping to popularise 
the idea that training the body should be 
a part of school learning. In the middle of 
the 19th century, many schools in Europe

Colonial India

focus was on literary subjects. The famous Wood’s Despatch of 1854 
laid the foundation for schools and universities but did not include 
physical education.

History and Culture of Physical Education in India
	
British sports like cricket, hockey, and football entered Indian 
schools and clubs, often promoted by the princely states and their 
rulers. Indian rulers like the Maharaja of Patiala and Maharaja of 
Vijyanagar patronised cricket and hockey, while wrestling and 
traditional games continued outside the school system.
	
In 1914, a major step was the foundation of the Hanuman 
Vyayam Prasarak Mandal (HVPM) in Amravati, Maharashtra. 
In 1920, Harry Crowe Buck, an American educator, started 
the YMCA College of Physical Education in Madras, which 
became the first professional institute for Physical Education in 
India. During the 1930s, more colleges opened in Hyderabad, 
Lucknow, and Bombay.

On the first day of fitness week at school, the Physical Education teacher asked 
the students to start with a short run. Ravi completed the lap quickly. Aisha 
ran at a steady pace without stopping. Anurag could not run very fast, but he 
balanced extremely well during a yoga pose. Observing all the students, the 
teacher smiled and said, “All of you are fit in different ways. It is not the same 
for every person.”
	
Being fit is not only about speed or strength. It is about balance, flexibility, 
endurance and confidence.
Types of Fitness and Their Components
Fitness is the ability of the body to work efficiently, stay 
healthy and perform daily activities without getting tired. A 
fit student can run, jump, lift, play and concentrate better 
in school. Fitness is not only about strength or long-distance 
running. It includes different types of abilities that help 
the body move smoothly and stay active. There are two 
main types of fitness, namely, Health-related Fitness and 
Skill-related Fitness. Both are important for students because 
they support overall growth, sports performance and 
well-being.
Fitness and its Components
Chapter 4
Health-related Fitness Components
Health-related fitness components help maintain good health, 
prevent diseases, and support everyday activities. They are important 
for all students, whether they play sports or not.


25
Fitness and its Components
Cardiovascular Endurance
It is the ability of the heart and lungs to supply oxygen to the body during 
physical activity. A student with good endurance can run, cycle or swim 
for a long time without getting tired. It includes jogging for 10 minutes 
during warm-up, skipping rope, or running a long-distance race.
Muscular Strength
It is the ability of a muscle to overcome resistance which could be in 
the form of body weight, air, water or any other objects. It is required 
for lifting, pushing, holding or carrying objects like books, doing 
push-ups, and holding a yoga posture.
Skill-related Fitness Components
These components help students perform better in games and sports. 
They support speed, coordination, and quick movement. However, 
skill-related fitness is not only for sports. It is an important part of 
daily life, physical literacy, and overall development. While players 
use these skills in sports, every person uses them in day-to-day 
activities. They help us stay active, independent, and confident.
Muscular Endurance
It is the ability of muscles to work for a long period without getting tired. 
It can be built by performing repeated sit-ups, holding Vṛikṣhāsana for 
20 seconds or cycling without stopping.
Flexibility
It is the ability of joints to move through a full range of motion. Flexible 
students can bend, stretch or turn without difficulty. Flexibility can 
be improved by performing forward bending in Yoga, stretching 
hamstrings, or movements in dance.
Body Composition
It is the proportion of fat, muscle, and bone in the body. A healthy body 
composition supports better movement and prevents lifestyle diseases. 
Balanced diet and regular physical activity help maintain a healthy body 
composition.

Agility is the ability to change direction quickly while maintaining 
balance. For example, dodging in Kho-Kho or moving from side to 
side in badminton. It is also helpful while moving through a crowded 
market, avoiding obstacles or changing direction while walking.

Understanding Our Body
The scientific study of the structure of the human body is known 
as human anatomy. It is about understanding ‘what’ our body parts 
are and ‘where’ they are located. Physiology, on the other hand, is 
the study of how different organs and systems of the human body 
function to maintain life. While anatomy shows us the ‘what’ and 
‘where’, physiology explains the ‘how’ and ‘why’ of bodily functions.

JOINT
A joint (also called an articulation) is a location where two or more 
bones meet to form a connection that allows movement. Joints in 
the human body function as hinges, pivots, and sliding mechanisms, 
providing mobility and enabling human locomotion. Joints also 
provide structural support and stability to the skeletal system. They 
are classified below on the basis of structure and function.
Fig. 5.9: Synovial joint
(Freely moveable joint)
	
•	
Synovial joints are freely movable joints characterised 
by a fluid-filled cavity covered by a membrane known 
as the synovial membrane between articulating bones 
(for example, knee, shoulder, etc.). It helps in wrist 
gliding in gymnastics.
	
•	
Fibrous joint is a type of joint in the body in 
which bones are connected by a dense fibrous 
connective tissue, like collagen which provides 
strength and stability (for example, skull), with 
no direct movement (head protection in contact 
sports like boxing).

Cardiac Muscles
Cardiac muscles are a specialised type of muscles found only in the 
walls of the heart. It forms the thick middle layer of the wall of the 
heart known as the myocardium. Its structure is branched, striated 
and has a single central nucleus. These muscles possess autonomic 
function with self-stimulating properties, enabling heart to pump 
blood throughout the body continuously till death.

oxygen and exhaling carbon dioxide. It involves your lungs, chest 
muscles, and diaphragm. For example, when you take a deep breath

Respiration, on the other hand, happens at the cellular level. It is 
the chemical process in your cells where oxygen is used to release 
energy from food, producing carbon dioxide and water as waste 
products. This energy fuels your muscles during exercise.

takes oxygen-rich blood from the lungs and sends it back to the body. 
Each side has two parts— an atrium and a ventricle. The atria act as 
entryways and help fill the ventricles with blood. Then, the ventricles 
squeeze, pushing blood out with enough pressure to move it through 
the body. The heart also has a special system that controls its steady 
beat and sends signals to make the heart muscles work together. 
	
The function of the circulatory system is to meet the needs of 
the body’s tissues. It transports nutrients to them, removes waste 
products, carries hormones to different parts of the body, and helps 
maintain balanced conditions in the fluids present in the tissue so 
that cells can survive and work effectively.
Physical Characteristics of Circulation
Humans have a double circulatory system, meaning blood passes 
through the heart twice during one complete trip around the body. 
This ensures that oxygen-rich blood (oxygenated) is separated from 
oxygen-deficient blood (deoxygenated) and reaches the tissues 
efficiently. The circulatory system is divided into two main parts:


49
Cardiorespiratory System
1.	
Pulmonary Circulation
	
•	
The right side of the heart pumps oxygen-deficient blood to 
the lungs.
	
•	
In the lungs, carbon dioxide is removed and oxygen is added.
	
•	
The oxygen-rich blood returns to the left side of the heart.
2.	
Systemic Circulation (also called peripheral circulation)
	
•	
The left side of the heart pumps oxygen-rich blood to all the 
body’s tissues.
	
•	
Cells use oxygen and produce carbon dioxide as waste.
	
•	
Oxygen-deficient blood flows back to the right side of the heart.
The main functional parts of circulation are:
•	
Arteries: They carry blood 
under high pressure from the 
heart to the tissues. They have 
strong walls and blood moves 
through them quickly.
•	
Arterioles:

•	
Capillaries: They are tiny blood vessels where the exchange of 
nutrients, waste products, gases, and other substances happens 
between the blood and the fluid around the cells. They have very 
thin walls that allow small molecules to pass through easily.
•	
Venules: They are small vessels that collect blood from the 
capillaries and join together to form larger veins.
•	
Veins: They carry blood back to the heart. They have thin walls, 
low pressure, and act as storage areas (reservoirs) for blood at 
the same time allowing blood to flow quickly.

Circulation is a Complete Circuit
When the left side of the heart contracts, it pumps blood into the 
systemic circulation through the aorta. The aorta branches into 
smaller arteries, then into arterioles, and finally into capillaries. Each 
heartbeat causes blood vessels to expand, because they can stretch 
or are distensible. When the heart relaxes, the vessels spring back 
(recoil), keeping blood flowing to the tissues even between beats.
	
Blood leaving the tissues flows into small veins called venules, 
which join together to form larger veins. These veins carry the blood 
back to the right side of the heart.
	
The right side of the heart then pumps the blood into the 
pulmonary artery, which branches into smaller arteries, arterioles, 
and capillaries in the lungs. Here, oxygen is taken into the blood 
and carbon dioxide is removed. The blood then flows from the lung 
capillaries into venules, then into larger veins, and finally into the 
left atrium and left ventricle. From there, it is pumped again into the 
systemic circulation, completing the circuit.
Sound of the Heart
The normal sound of the heart is a ‘lub-dub’ rhythm, caused by the 
closing of the heart’s four valves in sequence. The ‘lub’ is the sound of 
the mitral and tricuspid valves closing, and the ‘dub’ is the sound of 
the aortic and pulmonary valves closing. An abnormal sound, called 
a heart murmur, can occur from turbulent blood flow, which can be 
caused by a leaky or stiff valve or other heart problems.
Lub (S1): The first heart sound, created by the closing of the mitral 
and tricuspid valves as the heart’s ventricles begin to contract. 
Dub (S2): The second heart sound, caused by the closing of the aortic 
and pulmonary valves as the ventricles finish contracting and relax. 
Lub-dub: The regular, repeating pattern doctors listen for when 
checking a person’s heart health.

DID YOU KNOW?
•	
The autonomic nervous system has two main parts. The sympathetic 
nervous system plays the most important role in controlling blood 
circulation, while the parasympathetic nervous system helps regulate 
the function of the heart.
•	
A change in blood flow in any part of the circulation causes a change 
in other body tissues, which means whenever we do exercises such as 
the leg press, the blood flow is much more in the lower body tissues as 
compared to the upper body. 
•	
Cardiac output is the amount of blood the heart pumps into the aorta 
every minute. It is also the amount of blood that flows through the 
body’s circulation, carrying oxygen, nutrients, and other substances to 
the tissues and removing waste products.
•	
In an average adult, the cardiac output is about 5 litres per minute. 
When measured relative to body size (per square metre of body 
surface area), it is called the cardiac index, which is about 3 litres per 
minute per square metre.

Blood Pressure
Blood pressure, also known as arterial pressure is the pressure of the 
blood inside the arteries. It is created when the heart pumps blood 
into the arteries, pushing it against the artery walls. This pressure 
helps move blood through the whole body so oxygen and nutrients 
can reach the tissues. It is measured in terms of:
•	
Systolic pressure: It is the pressure when the heart contracts and 
pushes blood out.
•	
Diastolic pressure: It is the pressure when the heart relaxes 
between beats.

Development
Development is not limited to size or the body composition; it can be 
both behavioural and biological. Such as the development of motor 
skills, or learning teamwork or controlling emotions during sports. 
Development is shaped not only by your biology but also by your 
experiences.
Maturation
Maturation is the progression of the body towards adulthood. It can 
happen at different ages, rates and times in almost all individuals. 
Even within one person, all systems do not mature at the same 
time—for example, our skeletal system matures much later than our 
reproductive system.

Protection
Rather than totally resting the injured area, think more about protecting 
it from further damage. A short period of rest immediately after an injury 
is helpful to unload the injured area but total rest should be limited and 
combined with optimal loading.
Optimal Loading
Early activity encourages early recovery. Progressive loading of your injury 
can help promote optimal healing. It can prevent delays in returning to 
normal that may develop with prolonged rest such as joint and muscle 
tightness or muscle wasting.
Ice
Applying ice may help to manage the swelling around your injured muscle 
or joint and it can help decrease some of the acute pain that you may be 
experiencing. Icing every few hours for up to 20 minutes at a time is great 
for the first few days.
Compression
Applying a compression bandage evenly to the affected area, may help 
reduce any swelling you may have. You can combine this with ice.
Elevation
You can elevate the area during short periods of rest, again to help with 
swelling and reduce the pain. Make sure the injured part is elevated above 
the level of your heart.

significance in uniting athletes and nations. The unit emphasises 
the importance of participation over winning, as expressed in the 
Olympic Creed, and encourages students to embrace the spirit of 
fair play, mutual respect, and global friendship. Through engaging 
activities, students learn how sports can transcend boundaries

Origin and Purpose
In 2004, Indian shooter Rajyavardhan Singh Rathore was honoured

This ancient tradition, where victors received olive crowns instead 
of gold medals, connected the modern Games back to their origins in 
ancient Olympia.
	
The Olympic Games began in ancient Greece nearly 2,800 years 
ago. According to historical and archaeological evidence, the first 
recorded Games took place in 776 BCE at a place called Olympia, in 
the western part of Greece. Olympia was not a large city but a sacred 
valley surrounded by hills and olive trees.
	
The main purpose of the Games was to honour Zeus, the chief 
God of the Greeks, through physical excellence and fair competition. 
Ancient writers like Pausanias and Herodotus described how the

The Ancient Olympic Games
Games were both a religious festival and a celebration of human 
strength and skill. The Greeks believed that the body and mind should 
work together in harmony. Winning an event was seen as a gift to the 
gods and a way to achieve arête, meaning excellence of character. 
The Games encouraged peace, respect, and moral discipline. They 
brought together people from different regions, united by a shared 
belief in effort, fairness, and honour. These early ideals of the ancient 
Olympics became the foundation for the modern Olympic Movement 
that began many centuries later.
Organisation and Events
The ancient Olympic Games were 
carefully organised and followed 
sacred traditions. The people of 
Elis, a small region near Olympia, 
were responsible for preparing 
and managing the Games. They 
acted as judges and were called 
the Hellanodikai, meaning ‘Judges of the Greeks’. They trained for 
ten months before the festival to ensure that all events were fair 
and that every athlete followed the rules. Only free-born Greek men 
were allowed to compete. Athletes came from different city-states, 
such as Athens, Sparta, and Corinth. Before the Games began, every 
athlete had to take an oath in front of the statue of Zeus, promising to 
play fairly and respect the rules. This oath showed that honesty and 
discipline were as important as strength and skill. 
	
The Games were held every four years and lasted for five days. 
The first recorded event was the stadion race, a short sprint of about 
192 metres. Over time, more events were added. These included:
•	
Running races of different distances, such as the diaulos (two 
laps) and dolichos (long-distance race)
•	
Wrestling and boxing, which tested strength and technique
•	
Pankration, a tough event combining wrestling and boxing
•	
Pentathlon, which included five events — running, long jump, 
discus throw, javelin throw, and wrestling
•	
Chariot racing, which was a highlight of the Games and took 
place in the hippodrome

Rewards and Olympic Ideals
Winning an event at the ancient Olympic Games was considered one 
of the greatest honours in Greek society. The prize itself was simple, 
but the respect it carried was enormous. Victors were crowned with 
a wreath made of wild olive leaves, taken from a sacred tree near the 
temple of Zeus. This wreath, called the kotinos, symbolised purity, 
hard work, and peace. Unlike today, the winners did not receive 
money, medals, or gifts at Olympia. The real reward was honour and 
fame. When champions returned to their cities, they were welcomed 
with great celebrations. Poets wrote songs about them, sculptors 
made their statues, and sometimes the city even granted them free 
meals for life. These rewards showed that victory was a service to 
one’s community, not just personal success.
	
The idea behind the Olympic victory was deeply moral and 
spiritual. Competing in the Games was not just about physical 
strength but also about courage, fairness, and self-control. Losing

discipline. The poet Pindar praised Olympic winners in his famous 
victory odes, describing how their effort and virtue reflected divine 
blessings. He wrote that true glory comes to those who combine 
skill with moral strength. This belief became one of the roots of the 
Olympic ideals — respect, excellence, and friendship — that continue 
even today. The ancient Greeks saw the Olympic Games as a path to 
harmony between body, mind, and spirit. This balance is still central 
to the modern Olympic Movement, which encourages athletes to play 
with joy, fairness, and honour rather than focusing only on victory.
Social and Cultural Impact
The Olympic Games were more than sports competitions. They were 
a festival of peace, culture, and learning that united the people of 
ancient Greece. Although Greek city-states often fought wars, they 
stopped all conflicts during the Olympic Truce so that athletes and 
visitors could travel safely to Olympia. This showed how sport could 
bring peace and friendship among people. The Games also became a 
meeting place for art, music, and knowledge. Poets and artists created 
songs, statues, and paintings to honour the athletes. The philosopher 
Pindar wrote poems praising the courage and fairness of Olympic 
winners. At the same time, teachers and thinkers discussed health, 
discipline, and good character, showing that body and mind should 
grow together.
	
The festival also honoured Zeus, the chief deity in ancient Greek 
mythology. His great temple at Olympia had a huge statue made by 
the sculptor Phidias, which became one 
of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient

Olympics, though lost for centuries, became the foundation for the 
modern movement that continues to inspire the world today.


In the late nineteenth century, a young French educator named Pierre de 
Coubertin dreamed of using sport to bring nations together. He believed that 
playing games could teach young people courage, discipline, and respect for 
others. While visiting ancient Olympia in Greece, Coubertin was deeply moved 
by its history and ideals. Standing among the ruins of the old stadium, he 
imagined the revival of the Olympic Games as a symbol of peace and friendship 
among nations. His dream soon began to take shape. In 1894, at a meeting held 
in Paris, Coubertin and delegates from twelve countries decided to establish 
the International Olympic Committee (IOC). They agreed to organise the first 
Modern Olympic Games in Athens, Greece, the birthplace of the ancient Games.
	
Two years later, in April 1896, the dream became reality. Athletes from 
13 countries gathered in Athens to compete in 43 events. The Greek athlete 
Spyridon Louis, a humble water carrier, became a national hero by winning 
the marathon. The Games were filled with excitement and hope, and they 
marked the rebirth of the Olympic spirit after nearly 1,500 years. From that 
moment, the Modern Olympic Games became a celebration of human effort 
and unity, carrying forward the ideals of Excellence, Friendship, and Respect 
from ancient Greece to the entire world.
Growth and Development
After the success of the first modern Olympics in 1896, the Games 
began to grow and attract more countries and athletes from around 
the world. The idea of peaceful competition inspired nations to join 
together through sport. Over time, new events, better facilities, 
and improved organisation made the Olympics the world’s largest 
sporting festival. The International Olympic Committee (IOC), 
founded by Pierre de Coubertin, became the main body to plan and 
guide the Games. Every four years, a new city was chosen as the 
host. The Second Games were held in Paris in 1900, followed by

India’s Journey in the Olympic Movement
India’s Olympic journey began in 1900, when Norman Pritchard, 
representing India, won two silver medals in athletics at the Paris 
Games. Later, India formed its own National Olympic Committee in 
1927 and participated as a team in the 1928 Amsterdam Olympics. 
That year, Indian men’s hockey team, led by Dhyan Chand, won the 
gold medal. It was the beginning of India’s golden era in hockey, 
when the country went on to win eight Olympic gold medals between 
1928 and 1980. Over the years, India expanded its presence in many 
sports. Many athletes reflected the true Olympic values of excellence, 
perseverance, and respect.

The Identity of the Olympic Movement
The Olympic Creed
The Olympic Creed is a short message that expresses the true 
meaning of the Games. It reminds athletes and everyone watching 
that participating with effort and courage is more important than 
winning. The Creed was first spoken by Bishop Ethelbert Talbot 
during the 1908 London Olympics and later adopted by Pierre de 
Coubertin, the founder of the modern Games. It reflects the spirit 
of fair play and personal excellence that Olympism stands for. The 
Olympic Creed says:
	
“The most important thing in the Olympic Games is not to win 
but to take part, just as the most important thing in life is not the 
triumph but the struggle. The essential thing is not to have conquered 
but to have fought well.”

The Olympic Symbol (Five Rings)
The Olympic symbol is made up of five interlaced rings— blue, yellow, 
black, green, and red— on a white background. These rings represent 
the union of the five continents and the meeting of athletes from 
all over the world at the Olympic Games. The symbol was designed 
by Pierre de Coubertin in 1913. He chose these colours since every 
national flag in the world contains at 
least one of them, showing the idea 
of universality and inclusion. The 
Olympic rings first appeared on a flag 
in 1914 and were officially introduced 
at the 1920 Antwerp Games. Since 
then, they have become the most 
recognised emblem of global peace 
and sportsmanship.
The Olympic Flag
The Olympic Flag is one of the most important symbols of the Olympic 
Games. It carries the five interlaced rings on a white background, 
representing peace, purity, and unity among nations. The flag was 
designed by Pierre de Coubertin in 1913 and officially presented in 
1914 at the Olympic Congress in Paris. However, it was first used 
during the 1920 Antwerp Olympic Games in Belgium. The white 
background stands for peace, while the five coloured rings show 
the coming together of athletes from the five continents in a spirit 
of friendship and fair play.  At every Olympic Games, the flag plays 
a central role at the Opening and Closing Ceremonies. It is raised 
when the Games begin and lowered at the end. The Olympic Charter 
states that the flag must always remain clean, untorn, and treated 
with respect. 
The Olympic Motto
The Olympic Motto expresses the spirit of constant effort, 
improvement, and unity among athletes and nations. The original 
motto, “Citius, Altius, Fortius”, is a Latin phrase that means “Faster, 
Higher, Stronger.” It was first proposed by Father Henri Didon, a

The Olympic Oath
The Olympic Oath is a promise made by the athletes, coaches, and 
judges during the Opening Ceremony of every Olympic Games. The 
first Olympic Oath was introduced at the 1920 Antwerp Games. It was 
written by Pierre de Coubertin and spoken by Belgian fencer Victor 
Boin on behalf of all athletes. Since then, the wording of the oath has 
been updated several times to reflect fairness, equality, and inclusion. 
During the ceremony, one athlete, one coach, and one judge from the 
host country hold a corner of the Olympic flag and take the oath. 
This tradition symbolises unity and shared responsibility among all 
participants. The current Olympic

“In the name of all competitors, 
I promise that we shall take part in 
these Olympic Games, respecting 
and abiding by the rules and in 
the spirit of fair play, inclusion 
and equality, together we stand in 
solidarity and commit ourselves 
to sport without doping, without 
cheating, without any form of 
discrimination.”
The Olympic Emblems
Every Olympic Games has its own official emblem, which acts as 
the visual identity of that edition of the Games. It usually combines 
the Olympic rings with symbols, colours, or designs that represent 
the host country’s culture, values, and vision. The emblem appears 
on flags, banners, uniforms, tickets, and souvenirs. It helps create a 
sense of unity and pride for both the athletes and the host nation. The

The Olympic Anthem
The Olympic Anthem is a song that expresses the spirit of peace, 
friendship, and unity of the Olympic Games. It is played during the 
Opening and Closing Ceremonies whenever the Olympic flag is raised 
or lowered. The anthem was composed by Spyros Samaras, a Greek 
musician, with lyrics written by Kostas Palamas, a famous Greek poet. 
It was first performed at the first Modern Olympic Games in 1896 in 
Athens, Greece.  For many years, different Olympic host cities used 
their own songs. In 1958, the IOC officially adopted the composition 
by Samaras and Palamas as the official Olympic Anthem. Since then, 
it has been performed in every Olympic Games in various languages 
and musical styles.

Measures to Promote Inclusiveness in Sports
Develop accessible sports infrastructure for all
It is important to construct accessible parks, playgrounds, and 
stadiums so that everyone, including children, the elderly, and 
those with disabilities, may use them. For example, gender-neutral 
restrooms, tactile walkways for the blind, wheelchair-accessible 
seating, and ramps.
Enforce equal opportunity and anti-discrimination policies
It must be specifically stated in the regulations that anyone can 
participate in sports, regardless of gender, disability, caste, religion, 
or socio-economic status. Strict anti-discrimination laws encourage 
fairness and respect. 
Promote grassroots and community-level sports 
programmes
Local or community-based sporting events may be planned where 
women, children, and excluded communities can participate 
without worrying about being judged. Street sports events, school-
level competitions, and community tournaments all help to make 
play more inclusive and accessible.
Provide inclusive training for coaches and officials
Sports officials, coaches, and referees play an essential role in 
creating a fair environment. To prevent bias, they should be trained 
in inclusive practices, such as assuring equal playing time, modifying 
rules for athletes with disabilities, and using positive language.
Run awareness and media campaigns promoting diversity
Featuring women athletes, para-athletes, and persons from various 
cultural backgrounds through posters, social media posts, and 
community announcements encourages society to respect inclusivity.

Disability Etiquettes: Respecting Everyone with Dignity
Disability etiquettes are about the correct ways of behaving with 
people who have disabilities. It teaches us to treat them with respect 
and kindness, without pity or overprotecting them. People with 
disabilities want to be seen as individuals first, not just for their 
disability. By following disability etiquette, we can make them feel 
included, confident, and equal in society.
What are General Etiquettes?
These 
are 
basic 
principles 
of 
respectful, 
courteous 
behaviour 
toward all individuals:
1.	
Be Respectful: Treat everyone 
with dignity and consideration.
2.	
Be Inclusive: Don’t assume 
people can or can’t do things 
based on appearance.
3.	
Ask Before Helping: Never 
assume someone needs help—
always ask first.
4.	
Use First-Person (when preferred): One example would be 
“person with a disability” instead of “disabled person” (though 
some prefer identity-first, like “autistic person”—so follow 
individual preferences).
5.	
Don’t Stare, Point, or Make Jokes: Avoid drawing attention to 
someone’s disability or differences.

Understanding etiquette is important, but how do we actually make

practical way to adapt any activity so everyone can participate.
	
The STEP Model is used to make activities inclusive and suitable 
for everyone, including persons with disabilities.

Swami Vivekananda once said, “You will be nearer to heaven through football 
than through the study of the Gītā.”
	
By this, he did not mean that reading the Bhagavad Gītā was unimportant. 
He wanted to teach young people that physical strength and good health 
are just as important as spiritual learning. According to him, a strong body 
helps to build a strong mind, and only a healthy and active person can truly 
understand and follow spiritual teachings.
	
Vivekananda encouraged the youth to play games, exercise, and stay fit, 
because physical development is the first step toward becoming confident, 
disciplined, and spiritually aware.
Youth Participation and Health in Sports
Many children and teenagers today participate in different sports, 
and participation is growing every year. Young athletes often train 
throughout the year and may focus on one particular sport. By the 
time they reach adolescence, some may even compete at national or 
international levels.
	
Preteens often dedicate numerous hours weekly to sports such as 
tennis, gymnastics, hockey, or football. They sometimes travel with 
their teams to compete against peers of similar skill levels. Engaging 
in sports from a young age offers multiple health advantages, 
including enhanced fitness, teamwork, and confidence.
	
However, intense training and competition can sometimes lead 
to injuries or health problems. Conditions like asthma, heart defects, 
or Type 1 diabetes may appear or be diagnosed for the first time 
when a child starts regular sports training. Because young bodies 
are still growing, it is important to:
•	
monitor health carefully,

ensure proper warm-up and rest,

prevent and manage injuries, and

With the right care and guidance, young athletes can enjoy sports

Growth, Performance, and Fair Play in Youth Sports
•	
Rapid Changes During Growth: During childhood and 
adolescence, the human body changes more than at any other 
time in life. As children grow, their height, weight, strength, and 
coordination develop at different speeds. These changes affect 
how well they perform in sports.
•	
Peak Performance and Maturity: Some famous athletes, like 
Simone Biles (gymnastics champion at 16) and Alina Zagitova 
(Olympic gold medalist at 15), reached great success in their 
teenage years. However, for most people, sports performance 
improves gradually through childhood and adolescence and 
reaches its peak in their early 20s.
	
In younger children (under 12), early and late physical maturity 
doesn’t make a big difference. But during adolescence, those who 
mature earlier often have an advantage in sports like football, 
hockey, and rugby, because they are usually stronger and faster 
than late-maturing players of the same age.
Aerobic and Anaerobic Capacity
•	
Aerobic Capacity means how well your heart and lungs work to 
supply oxygen during activity. It increases with growth and training.

•	
Anaerobic Capacity is the ability to use short bursts of power 
and energy, like sprinting or jumping. It also improves as the 
muscles grow stronger with age.
In boys and girls before puberty, strength levels are quite similar. 
During and after puberty, boys gain more muscle mass, which 
increases their overall strength and power.
Creating a Level Playing Field
Have you ever noticed that students of the same age can look very 
different in height, strength, and build? This is because everyone 
grows and develops at their own pace. In sports, this can sometimes 
create unequal competition. For example, one 13-year-old who 
matures early may be much taller and stronger than another 13-year-
old who is still growing. When such players compete against each 
other, it can be unfair and may even increase the risk of injury.

Early Specialisation: Good or Bad?
The idea of early specialisation—when a child focuses on just one 
sport from a very young age—has been debated for many years. 
Some coaches and teams believe that the earlier a child starts training 
seriously in one sport, the better chance they have of becoming a star 
athlete, like Lionel Messi in football or LeBron James in basketball.
	
However, many experts warn that early specialisation can 
have serious drawbacks. Training too much at too early can lead to 
injuries, stress, and burnout. It can also take away the joy of playing 
and reduce time for free play, rest, and learning other skills.
Recommendations 
•	
Play multiple sports: Children should be encouraged to try 
different sports before choosing the one that they enjoy the most.
•	
Include free play: Unstructured play, like running, climbing, 
or casual games, helps build coordination and motor skills 
naturally.
•	
Balance training: Young athletes should not train more hours 
per week than their age (for example, a 12-year-old shouldn’t 
train more than 12 hours a week).
•	
Avoid overtraining: Too much intense practice can cause 
injuries and mental exhaustion.
•	
Build strength safely: All young people can benefit from general 
fitness and strength-building exercises done under supervision.
•	
Stay safe: Coaches and parents must ensure young athletes are 
protected from any form of pressure, bullying, or abuse.
Risks of Early Specialisation
Early specialisation can lead to:
•	
Overuse injuries (because of repeating the same movements).
•	
Burnout or loss of interest.
•	
Social isolation and less time with friends.
•	
Overdependence on coaches or parents.
•	
Stunted growth or delayed development.


121
Young Athlete
	
Children should enjoy multiple sports, explore different 
activities, and develop overall physical and mental strength before 
choosing to specialise in one sport. Exploring a variety of games 
helps develop balanced skills, confidence, and fitness. In fact, many 
top athletes did not specialise early; they focused on one sport only 
in their teenage years and often achieved better performance than 
those who specialised too soon.

Physical Benefits of Sports for Women
Regular participation in sports and physical activity provides 
numerous health benefits for women, including:
Prevention of Lifestyle Diseases
Regular physical activity reduces the risk of diabetes, high blood 
pressure, and obesity, helping women maintain good health 
throughout life.
Stronger Bones and Muscles
Women are more likely to suffer from osteoporosis (weak bones) 
than men. Sports and exercise help increase bone density and keep 
the skeleton strong.
Improved Muscle Tone
Regular training and sports participation strengthen muscles, 
improve flexibility, and help maintain proper posture.
Better Heart and Lung Health
Exercise improves blood circulation and oxygen supply to muscles. It 
strengthens the heart and allows women to perform physical activities 
for longer without fatigue.
Weight Control
Obesity is a major health concern, and women are more likely to be 
affected than men. Active participation in sports helps maintain a 
healthy weight and body shape.

Menarche – The Beginning of Menstrual Cycle
Adolescence is a stage of life marked by rapid physical and biological 
changes that lead to sexual maturity. For girls, one of the most 
important phases of this stage is menarche, or the first menstrual 
period.
	
Menarche usually occurs between the ages of 8 and 15 years, 
though this can vary for every individual. It marks the time when a 
girl’s body becomes capable of reproduction. This process is a natural 
part of growth and is linked with the development of secondary 
sexual characteristics, such as breast growth and widening of pelvic 
girdle.
Many factors can affect the timing of menarche, including:
•	
Genetics (family history)
•	
Nutrition and health
•	
Physical activity and body weight
•	
Socio-economic and environmental conditions

Understanding Menstruation
Menstruation (often called a “period”) is the monthly shedding of the 
uterine lining through the vagina.
•	
Periods typically last 3 to 7 days, though more commonly 
3 to 5 days.
•	
A normal menstrual cycle occurs every 21 to 35 days.
•	
Women typically have periods until the age of 45–55 years, after 
which they experience menopause, meaning the natural end of 
menstruation and fertility.
Menstrual health is an important part of overall well-being. Regular 
cycles are a sign of a healthy reproductive system.
Menstrual Dysfunction
Sometimes, women and young girls experience irregularities or 
problems in their menstrual cycle. These are called menstrual 
dysfunctions.
	
A normal menstrual cycle happens once every 21–35 days. If it 
occurs more often, less often, or causes extreme pain or bleeding, it 
may indicate a problem.
Common Causes
•	
Overweight or underweight
•	
Stress and anxiety
•	
Poor diet or eating disorders
•	
Sudden changes in physical activity or training schedules
•	
Hormonal imbalance or certain illnesses
Types of Menstrual Disorders
Pre-Menstrual Syndrome (PMS)
•	
Emotional and physical symptoms before periods such as mood 
swings, headaches, tiredness, and irritability.
•	
Can be managed through exercise, healthy diet, and enough rest.


127
Women in Sports
Amenorrhea
•	
Primary Amenorrhea: When menstruation does not start by 
puberty.
•	
Secondary Amenorrhea: When a woman misses her periods 
for three months or more after they have started.
Dysmenorrhea
•	
Painful periods with cramps, backache, or fatigue.
Menorrhagia
•	
Very heavy or prolonged bleeding during periods.
Polymenorrhea
•	
Periods occurring too frequently (less than 21 days apart).
Oligomenorrhea
•	
Infrequent periods, more than 35 days apart.
Metrorrhagia
•	
Irregular bleeding between periods.
Postmenopausal Bleeding
•	
Bleeding that happens after menopause (after a woman has 
stopped menstruating).
The Female Athlete Triad
While sports bring many health benefits for girls and women, 
some athletes face health issues due to imbalanced diet and training.
	
One such condition is called the Female Athlete Triad, first 
described by the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) 
in 1992.
The triad includes three interrelated conditions:
1.	
Low Energy Availability (with or without eating disorder)
2.	
Menstrual Dysfunction (absence or irregularity of periods)
3.	
Low Bone Mineral Density (weak bones)
These three are connected and often occur together in athletes 
who train intensely but don’t eat enough to meet their body’s 
energy needs.


128
Khel Praveen
Low Energy Availability
This happens when athletes do not eat enough food to balance the 
energy they spend during exercise.
•	
It may occur due to dieting, fear of weight gain, or eating 
disorders such as:
	
–	
Anorexia Nervosa: An extreme fear of gaining weight, 
leading to eating very little or over-exercising.
	
–	
Bulimia Nervosa: Eating large amounts of food and then 
using unhealthy methods to remove calories, such as 
vomiting or using laxatives.
•	
Common symptoms include fatigue, weight loss, stress fractures, 
irregular periods, anxiety, and weakness.
	
Proper nutrition and professional guidance can prevent or 
correct this condition.
Low Bone Mineral Density (BMD)
Bone health depends on proper nutrition, hormones, and exercise. 
When women have low oestrogen levels or poor diet, bones become 
fragile and weak, a condition known as osteoporosis.
•	
This can cause stress fractures, pain, or deformity later in life.
•	
Bone growth and strengthening happen mainly during childhood 
and adolescence, so maintaining proper diet and physical activity 
during these years is vital.
	
Sports are not just about winning medals or breaking records. 
For women, they represent freedom, empowerment, and the 
opportunity to challenge stereotypes. Every girl who picks up 
a cricket bat, laces up running shoes, or steps onto a basketball 
court is making a statement about her capabilities and her right 
to participate fully in society.
Myths and Taboos
A common myth is that girls should not exercise during their period. 
But in truth, light exercise often helps reduce cramps and improve 
mood. Even Olympic champions compete during menstruation. 
What matters is comfort, hygiene, and listening to your own body.

Fauja Singh was a British Sikh marathon runner of Indian origin and an 
inspiration for people of all ages. He was born on April 1, 1911, in Beas Pind 
village, Jalandhar, Punjab. During his childhood, he was weak and could not 
walk properly until the age of five. He worked as a farmer for most of his
early life.
	
After the death of his wife and son, Fauja Singh moved to England and 
took up running to overcome his grief. At the age of 89, he started training 
seriously and completed his first marathon in 2000 at the London Marathon. 
He went on to run marathons in London, Toronto, and New York.
	
In 2011, at the age of 100, Fauja Singh became the first centenarian to 
complete a full marathon at the Toronto Waterfront Marathon. He also set 
multiple world age-group records in track events. Known as the “Turbaned 
Tornado”, he promoted fitness culture through sports.
	
Fauja Singh retired from competitive running at the age of 101. His life 
proves that age is not a barrier to physical fitness and that regular exercise 
promotes both physical and mental well-being.
Have you noticed that many people today stay active and energetic 
even as they grow older? In recent years, the average age of 
participants in endurance sports such as long-distance running, 
cycling, and swimming has increased. Many older athletes train 
regularly, follow healthy routines, and set personal goals to keep 
themselves motivated. Their example shows that with the right 
attitude and care, the human body can remain strong and active for 
decades. It reminds us that staying active keeps both the body strong 
and the mind fresh—age may bring changes, but it does not have to 
limit our abilities or dreams.
	
As people grow older, the body naturally changes. Muscle 
strength, balance, and energy levels may gradually decrease, and 
some people become less active. This can increase the risk of health

problems such as heart disease, diabetes, obesity, and weak bones. 
However, regular physical activity can slow down these changes and 
help older adults live longer, healthier, and more independent lives. 
That is why exercise is often called “the best medicine for ageing”.
	
Staying active in later years does not always mean playing 
competitive sports. It can include simple and enjoyable activities like 
walking, swimming, yoga, cycling, gardening, or light exercises at 
home. The key is to keep moving regularly and choose activities that 
feel enjoyable and safe.
	
Healthy ageing means living an active, independent, and 
meaningful life as we grow older. It involves maintaining physical 
fitness, mental alertness, and emotional well-being so that people can 
continue doing the things they value — such as travelling, meeting 
friends, or playing with grandchildren. This depends not only on 
a person’s own health and habits but also on having a supportive 
environment that encourages activity and social connection.

Safety and Precautions
Older adults should always consult a doctor before starting a new 
exercise programme, especially if they have heart or joint problems. 
Some safety tips include:
•	
Start slowly and increase the intensity and frequency of the 
activity gradually.
•	
Warm up and cool down properly.
•	
Stay hydrated and wear comfortable shoes.
•	
Avoid very heavy or high-impact exercises.
•	
Exercise regularly, not occasionally.
•	
Doing light activity consistently brings great benefits.

This unit introduces students to the wide and exciting world of sports and 
physical activities, highlighting their importance in maintaining a healthy 
and active lifestyle. It begins by focusing on India’s rich heritage of indigenous 
martial arts such as Thang-Ta, which combines body movements, breathing 
techniques, and weapon skills to develop physical control and mental 
balance. These traditional practices reflect cultural values and promote 
discipline and self-defence.
	
The unit further explores various traditional Indian sport and martial 
arts like Mallakhamb, Gatka, and Kalaripayattu. These activities not only 
improve strength, flexibility, and coordination but also teach focus, courage, 
and respect. They serve as an important link between physical training and 
cultural traditions, helping students understand the significance of India’s 
historical sporting practices.
	
In addition, the unit covers modern and international 
sports, including combat sports like Judo, Wrestling, 
Taekwondo, and Boxing, as well as popular games 
like Hockey, Basketball, and Cricket. It also introduces 
individual sports like Archery, Tennis, explaining their 
fundamental skills.
	
Overall, this unit aims to develop awareness about the 
role of sports in building physical fitness, mental strength, 
teamwork, and sportsmanship. It encourages students to 
actively participate in different sports and appreciate their 
value in personal development, cultural understanding, 
and leading a balanced and healthy life.

Gymnastics
The word gymnastics has been derived from the Greek word Gymnos 
meaning “naked art”. In ancient Greek, the word Gymnastics was 
used to denote the exercise done with the naked body. Friedrich 
GutsMuths, is known as the Grand Father of Modern Gymnastics 
and Friedrich Ludwig Jahn is known as the Father of Gymnastics. 
Modern gymnastics is regulated by the Federation International 
de Gymnastique (FIG) which was founded in 1881. Gymnastics 
was included in the Modern Olympic Games in 1896 and women’s 
gymnastics was introduced in Olympic Games in 1928. The 
first gymnastics World Cup was organised in 1975. At present, 
gymnastics as an event can be seen in almost all prominent multi
sports meets.
Types of Gymnastics
Broadly gymnastics has been classified in seven types:
•  Artistic Gymnastics	
	
	
•  Parkour Gymnastics
•  Rhythmic Gymnastics 	
	
•  Tumbling Gymnastics
•  Trampoline Gymnastics	
	
•  Aerobics Gymnastics
•  Acrobatic Gymnastics	
This chapter deals with artistic gymnastics which is a popular 
discipline of gymnastics. Artistic gymnastics apparatus for men are 
floor exercise, pommel horse, roman rings, vaulting table, parallel 
bars, and horizontal bar. For women, the apparatus are vaulting 
table, uneven bars, balancing beam and floor exercises.

Thang-Ta
A fine combination of body, breath and blade is an indigenous martial 
art of Manipur in Northeast India, where thang stands for sword 
and ta stands for spear. Various 
external weapons are integrated 
in this skill i.e. the sword, spear, 
dagger, etc. — it’s a manifestation 
of physical control through soft 
movements 
coordinated 
with 
the rhythms of breathing. It is 
an elaborate system of physical 
culture that involves breathing 
methods, 
meditations, 
and 
traditions. The other name for 
Thang-Ta is Huyen Lallong (method of safe-defense). Any individual 
all alone or with a partner can practice various sword drills for 
learning and training fundamental strokes and footwork.
Skills
Basic Stance – Stand with feet shoulder width apart.
Footwork – Turn to the left, pivoting on the balls of both feet. The feet 
should form an approximately forty-five degree angle. The body will 
bend in front until the toes of the left foot are aligned with the knee 
and the chin. The body of the player should form a straight line from 
the back of the head down to the right heel. This is also called the 
Singh Mudrā or Lion’s Posture.
	
Another skill of thang ta which is played without any armory is 
known as Sarik Sarat it is taught after competence in weapons was 
gained. It uses the foot and hand as a form of weapon.

Gatka
Derived from the Sikh history, Gatka (refers to a wooden stick whose 
traditional name is Soti intended to simulate a sword) is an ancient 
Indian martial art which remained an integral part of the traditional 
Guru Parampara in Sikh Shastra Vidhya (skill to use weapon). During 
the 16th century the Sixth Guru Hargobind sahib Ji (1595–1644) 
initiated this historical art of self-defense. Later in the 17th century, 
Guru Gobind Singh ji, known as master of weaponry, developed it 
further and made it a compulsory part of the Sikh tradition. The word 
Gatka is of Indian origin and refers to a defensive weapon technique. 
Mahan Kosh edited by Bhai Kahan Singh Nabha, explains; Gatka a 
three-hand span stick, used to teach the initially of club fighting. 
Gatka associated with the Sikh Gurus, is now being recognised as a 
sport nationwide. Gatka is a combative sport between two or more 
players. RASMI (symbolic and traditional) and KHEL (competitive 
sport) are the two ways to enjoy this traditional art of sport reformed 
in the beginning of 19th century. International Rules for Gatka games 
are formulated by the World Gatka Federation. Gatka equipment 
used in demonstrations and competitions are stick and farri, chakra 
and chain, dhaal and kripaan, khanda and kataar, talwar and mace, 
teer and dang, etc.

Kalaripayattu
Kalari means a space where one gets training and Payattu means 
practising. References cited in the Vedas prove that Kalaripayattu 
has existed for 3,000 years, with deep roots in the social and cultural 
life of Keralam. Like yoga āsanas, Kalaripayattu is also inspired by 
the movements and stances of animals such as boar, lion, raptors, 
elephants, etc., and was primarily developed to counter wildlife. The 
British banned the practice, fearing it might make Indian citizens 
physically strong, emotionally balanced and mentally stable. Despite 
such restrictions, a few dedicated masters safeguarded this ancient 
art by practising it in isolation and secrecy, and it was resurrected 
after independence.
	
With strong principles at its foundation, Kalaripayattu develops 
balance and flexibility of mind and body, emphasising speed, agility, 
co-ordination and discipline. It also aids in reducing psychological 
disorders like stress, anxiety, and tension.
	
Traditionally, advanced students of Kalaripayattu are imparted 
knowledge of marma-sthānam — the vital points of the body, which 
include muscular, vascular, ligamentous, bone and joint-related 
locations. This deepens their understanding of the body’s functioning 
and is complemented by medicinal and therapeutic practices that 
are integral to the training.

Indigenious Sport
Mallakhamb
Mallakhamb is a Indian traditional game that beautifully 
blends strength, flexibility, balance, and concentration. 
The term comes from two Sanskrit words — Malla meaning 
wrestler, and Khamb meaning pole — thus translating to 
“Wrestler’s Pole”. Originating in ancient India, 
Mallakhamb was an essential part of the 
Gurukul-era physical training system, designed 
to build strength, focus, and discipline among 
students. It served as a vital link between 
Yoga, martial arts, and wrestling, combining 
physical 
mastery 
with 
spiritual 
growth. 
Practiced in Gurukuls and Akharas, it helped warriors and 
wrestlers enhance agility, endurance, and coordination 
while promoting inner balance and mental control. 
	
Historically, Mallakhamb finds mention in ancient 
Sanskrit texts such as Manasollasa (12th century AD) and 
Vyayam Dipika, highlighting its deep cultural roots. It was 
revived during the reign of Bajirao Peshwa II (1796–1818) by his 
court gymnast Balambhat Dada Deodhar, to train soldiers for battle. 
During the early 20th century, stalwarts like R. V. Bhogle, K. B. 
Boksekar, and Pt. Babasaheb Natu popularised it across India. 
	
Today, Mallakhamb has evolved into a modern competitive and 
artistic sport, performed by both men and women on wooden poles, 
ropes, or hanging apparatuses, where yogic and acrobatic postures 
are displayed with rhythm and grace. Mallakhamb helps in the 
all-round development of the body and mind. It improves muscular 
strength and endurance, balance and co-ordination, flexibility and 
agility, concentration and self-control, courage and confidence.
	
Mallakhamb has gained official recognition — declared the 
State Sport of Madhya Pradesh (2013) and featured in the Khelo 
India Games and 36th National Games (2022). Today, it has spread 
globally to countries like Germany, the USA, Japan, Italy, and the UK, 
symbolising India’s ancient wisdom in a modern athletic form with 
minimum cost.


Judo
Judo has its origin in the ancient Japanese art of (Jiu-Jitsu) (gentle 
art), a system of hand-to-hand combat. It is a sport of Asian origin 
which was included in Tokyo Olympic Games in 1964. The first school 
of Judo was started by Professor Jigoro Kano in 1882 at Eishoji (a 
Buddhist temple) in Tokyo, Japan. As a player progresses through the 
ranks (grading reflected by the color of the belt of the Judo player) 
and the very nature of the grading system ensures that the next goal 
is always realistic and achievable with effort.
	
The Judo bouts are played in different weight categories for 
equality and to avoid injuries because body weight has direct 
relation with body inertia, mass and strength (force) of an individual. 
Competitions are held in sub-junior boys/girls, junior boys/girls and 
senior men/women with different weight categories.

Combat Sports
Taekwondo
Taekwondo is a Korean martial-arts-turned-olympic-sport practiced 
globally. Through consistent practice, this discipline helps people 
improve their spirit and quality of 
life by strengthening both the body 
and the mind. The deeper meaning 
of Taekwondo is reflected in the term 
itself: Tae means foot or to step on, 
Kwon means fist or fight, and Do means 
the way or discipline. When combined, 
they symbolise the skill of using one's 
fists and feet in a controlled way to 
promote self-control and peace as 
opposed to violence. Fundamentally, 
Taekwondo teaches that harmony 
and peace are the ultimate aims of 
both martial and human development.
	
Taekwondo, originally called Subak or Taekkyon, originated in 
Korea and was used as a physical training and self-defence technique. 
Later, during the Koguryo kingdom, it evolved into Sunbae, and 
during the Shilla period, it joined Hwarangdo, which concentrated 
on creating honourable leaders for the country. Taekwondo evolved 
during 1940s and 1950s in Korea from ancient Korean martial arts 
practiced during the Three-Kingdom era and was formalised through 
the establishment of the World Taekwondo Federation (WTF) in 1973 
later renamed as World Taekwondo (WT).

Softball
Softball is said to have begun — indoors, 
actually — in 1887 on Thanksgiving Day 
in Chicago. A group of men had gathered 
together at Chicago’s Farragut Boat Club. 
During the merriment a reporter from 
the Chicago Board of Trade, George 
Hancock, tied up a boxing glove (with 
its own strings) into a sphere, took a 
broomstick handle, and marked lines on 
the floor using chalk. That night 80 runs 
were scored in the game that took place 
and thus the sport was born. The first 
rulebook is said to have been issued (by 
Hancock) in 1889.
	
In 1895, Lewis Rober Sr. moved the game to a vacant lot outside 
the Minneapolis, Minnesota firehouse he worked at, so that the 
firefighters could get some exercise while waiting for an alarm. 
It was known at that time as ‘kitten league ball,’ which was later 
shortened to ‘kitten ball.’ In 1922 the name ‘kitten ball’ was first 
changed to ‘diamond ball.’ In 1926 the term ‘softball’ was used, when 
Walter Hakanson of the YMCA (Young Men’s Christian Association) of 
Denver, Colorado conceived it while attending a meeting to form the 
Colorado Amateur Softball Association. In 1933 the first-ever national 
amateur softball tournament took place in conjunction with Chicago 
World’s Fair and soon after, the reporter who wrote about the event, 
Leo Fischer, helped establish the Amateur Softball Association (ASA). 
In 1952 the first meeting was held for the International Softball 
Federation (ISF), which would govern the sport around the world, 
and the first world championship took place in 1965, when women’s 
teams from five countries competed in Australia. One year later, the 
first Men’s World Championship was played in Mexico.
