Yoga is a continuous journey of awareness and self-development. By the end of 
Grade 8, we explored the values of Yama and Niyama, learned to move our body 
gently through Sūkṣhma-vyāyāma, understood the importance of practising 
Sūrya-namaskāra regularly, and became familiar with Āsanas that keep the 
body steady and comfortable. We also developed breath awareness through 
prāṇāyāma, learned to guide our senses wisely with Pratyāhāra, and discovered 
how to focus the mind through Dhāraṇā and Dhyāna. With this strong and 
balanced foundation, we are now ready to deepen our yogic journey and 
understand these practices with greater clarity. In Grade 9, we will build on 
everything we have learned so far, deepening our understanding and practice. 
	
The uniqueness of Unit 6 lies in its simple and practical approach to the 
three main themes, Samagra Svāsthya (Yoga for Holistic Health), Yogamaya 
Jīvana (Lifestyle for Holistic Health) and Yoga for Personal Excellence.
	
Samagra Svāsthya introduces the definition of health, meaning of 
holistic health, the states of ease and disease, as well as the various dimensions 
of health.
	
Yogamaya Jīvana highlights different lifestyle factors such as āhāra 
(food), nidrā (sleep), brahmacharya (moderation), good company, behaviour, 
circadian rhythm, and habit formation. It offers simple tools to help students 
manage the challenges of adolescence. The chapter also shows how yoga 
improves focus, emotional balance, confidence, and menstrual health. 
	
Yoga for Personal Excellence guides students to choose the path of 
Śhreyas  (long-term excellence) over Preyas (temporary pleasure). 
Through the inspiring stories of contemporary role models, students 
learn that true success is achieved by connecting hard work to a 
‘higher goal’ beyond oneself. Finally, it provides a practical Yoga toolkit, 
including breathwork and self-reflection, to help students transform their 
scattered energy into a focused force for personal and national growth.

Samagra Svāsthya
Definition of Health
Many people equate being healthy as simply a state of ‘not being 
sick’. However, consider a student who never falls ill but consistently 
feels lonely and stressed. Are they really healthy? The answer is 
‘no’. According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), health is 
defined as, “a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-
being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity”. In 
other words, health is a condition where there is proper balance and 
harmony among all the dimensions, not just the absence of diseases. 
	
Being healthy is having the energy to play and study, a calm 
mind to think clearly, and the happiness of being connected with 
others. Therefore, health is more than just ‘not being sick’, it is about 
living a balanced life where the body, mind, and social bonds all 
work together in harmony.
	
Health can be interpreted in the dimensions given below.
Dimensions
What it covers
Physical
Eating nutritious food, getting regulated sleep, 
regular exercise, good hygiene and self-care, proper 
functioning of all body systems, good strength, 
stamina and flexibility.
Mental

Staying calm, confident and resilient, being able to 
cope with life’s stresses and challenges, showing 
empathy and understanding others, being able to 
regulate emotions like joy, fear, anger and sadness 
and being free from attachment and aversion.
Social
Building good relationships with family and friends, 
working together cooperating, sharing, caring and 
communicating, being responsible in the community, 
and living with a positive attitude, truthfulness, 
nonviolence, selflessness, and contentment.

Ease and ‘Dis-Ease’
Let us understand the state of Svāsthya from the yogic point of view. 
The term Svāstha (health) signifies being rooted in one’s own natural 
state of balance, a state of ease and harmony. ‘Sva’ means self and 
‘stha’ means being established. It is the natural state of the body to 
remain in balance. Though the body constantly strives to maintain 
an equilibrium, our lifestyle, habits, and stressful experiences can 
disturb this state, leading to Vyādhi (disease).

when the body and mind are changing rapidly.
	
Adolescence (10–19 years) is a dynamic and important stage of life — a 
time of fast growth, changing emotions, and new responsibilities. It is also full 
of curiosity and creativity, as we begin to discover who we are becoming. The 
habits we build during this stage shape the adults we will grow into. Yoga can 
be a strong and steady support during this time, helping maintain physical 
strength, emotional balance, and mental clarity.
	
In today’s world, we often think of lifestyle in terms of convenience, fast 
food, lots of screen time, and quick shortcuts. But living a meaningful lifestyle 
is really about small habits that help us stay healthy and balanced. In this 
chapter, we will look at a few simple factors that shape our well-being, such as:
•	
Āhāra — what and how we eat
•	
Nidrā — the quality of our sleep
•	
Brahmacharya — how wisely we use our time and channelise our 
	
energy
•	
Satsaṅga (Good Company)— having friends who inculcate Sadguna 
(good habits) ultimately leading to character building.
•	
Sadvyavahāra (Positive Behaviour) — our actions and responses in  
	
daily life
•	
Jaivika-ghaṭī (Circadian Rhythm) — the body’s natural day–night cycle
•	
Saṁskāra-nirmāṇa (Habit Formation) — the routines we follow 
	
every day
	
Understanding these factors helps us build a lifestyle that 
supports good health in body, mind, and emotions.
Āhāra (food)
Food is not just something we eat to satisfy our hunger or for taste, 
it becomes part of our body and influences how we think, feel, and 
act. As you have learnt in science, every cell in our body is formed 
from the food we consume. In yogic tradition, food is therefore 
considered an important factor in shaping both physical health and 
mental clarity. The Bhagavad-Gītā (Chapter 17) classifies food into 
three broad types — Sāttvika, Rājasika, and Tāmasika.


165
Yogamaya Jīvana
Sāttvika food includes fresh fruits, vegetables, 
grains, nuts, milk, and ghee. These are light, 
nourishing, and best when eaten fresh. They 
help keep the mind calm, improve concentration, 
and support overall health and balance. Sāttvika 
food purifies the body and mind, and supports 
longevity. This category primarily includes foods 
that are freshly prepared, juicy, and natural.
Rājasika food includes items that are spicy, sour, 
very hot, salty, or stimulating, such as strong 
spices, fried snacks, tea, coffee, and fast food. 
When eaten in moderation, these can provide 
energy and strength, which may be beneficial 
for growing children and adolescents. However, 
excessive intake can lead to restlessness, 
impatience, irritation, or reduced focus. Rājasika 
food stimulates both the body and the mind.
Tāmasika food includes stale, reheated, heavily 
processed, or leftover foods, along with the items 
that have been stored for a long time. Such foods 
tend to make the body feel heavy and the mind 
dull, reducing alertness, enthusiasm, and clarity.
	
For students, a balanced diet mainly includes sāttvika foods, 
with rājasika food in moderation for activity and growth, while 
avoiding excessive tāmasika foods. Making mindful food choices 
helps maintain energy, focus, and a healthy lifestyle.
	
As the famous proverb states, ‘Yathā annaṁ tathā manaḥ’ which 
means ‘As is the food, so is the mind’. This highlights the close 
connection between what we eat and how we think and feel.


166
Khel Praveen
Mitāhāra: Moderate Eating
Yoga reminds us that how we eat matters just as 
much as what we eat. Eating in moderation is a vital 
discipline in yoga. Mitāhāra emphasises pleasant, 
nourishing food taken with reverence, leaving one-
fourth of the stomach empty to maintain lightness 
and balance. When food is received as prasāda, 
accompanied by chanting and a mental offering 
to the Divine, it becomes a source of purification 
rather than indulgence. As the Haṭha-Yoga-
Pradīpikā (1.58) states, “Mitāhāra, moderate 
eating, is conducive to yoga. It consists of 
pleasant and nourishing food, leaving one-fourth of 
the stomach empty, eaten as an offering to Śhiva.”
	
Awareness is the gateway to transforming our relationship 
with food. Eating slowly and without distractions, allows us to truly 
taste and enjoy each bite, while also noticing the moment we feel 
comfortably full. In contrast, packaged and processed foods are 
deliberately designed to trigger cravings, often disturbing our energy, 
mood, sleep, and focus. By cultivating mindful eating, observing 
hunger signals, eating with calm attention, and noticing how food 
affects our body and mind we protect ourselves from unhealthy 
addictions and naturally move towards choices that nourish us.

According to the Patañjali’s Yoga-sūtra, deep sleep is a state in 
which the mind experiences the awareness of ‘nothing’, giving it 
complete rest. Modern science also emphasises that teenagers need 
quality sleep for healthy functioning, yet many miss this due to late-

ways to care for both the body and mind.
	
Just as the food we eat influences our mind and the sleep we

also shapes our overall well-being. This is where brahmacharya 
becomes meaningful. While āhāra nourishes the body and 
nidrā refreshes the mind, brahmacharya teaches us to use our
time and energy wisely so we can stay focused, balanced, and healthy 
in our everyday life.

Brahmacharya
Brahmacharya is one of the Yamas in yoga and refers to the wise 
use of your time and energy. Adolescence is traditionally known as 
Brahmacharyāśhrama, a stage of life meant for learning, building 
discipline, and developing good values. Practising brahmacharya 
will help you manage your senses, reduce unnecessary distractions, 
and stay focused on what truly matters in life.
	
In everyday life, this simply means noticing where your time 
and energy are utilised. Sometimes we may spend too much time on 
social media, gaming, eating junk food, etc. These habits drain our 
energy. A small step toward brahmacharya is to observe our routines 
honestly and bring moderation into one area at a time. Even small 
changes can make a big difference.
	
Brahmacharya is not about strict rules, it is about balance. It 
teaches us to direct our energy towards things that help us learn, 
grow, and become the best version of ourselves. It is the control over 
the temptations of your mind that can deviate us from your aim. It 
is the preservation and direction of our energy and time towards 
righteousness.
	
Our social and emotional well-being connects naturally with 
brahmacharya. When we use our time and energy wisely, it becomes 
easier to handle emotions, avoid distractions, and build positive 
relationships with the people around us.

spiritual living and inspired people across the world. He emphasised 
that brahmacharya is not only for monks, and students too can 
practise it by avoiding distractions and using their time wisely. His 
life shows us that small habits of balance and self-discipline help us 
stay focused, use our energy well, and achieve meaningful goals.

Satsanga (Good Company)
The people we spend time with play an important role in shaping 
our thoughts, habits, and the way we use our energy. This good 
company is referred to as Satsaṅga, or good company. Being around 
positive, disciplined, and supportive people makes it easier to 
practise brahmacharya. Great yogis like Swami Sivananda and Swami 
Vivekananda, through their lives, set an example that good company 
helps us stay focused, channel our energy towards meaningful work, 
and grow steadily without feeling pressurised. Their experiences 
remind us that choosing the right company is a quiet but powerful 
step towards personal growth and balance.

Circadian Rhythm and Habit Formation
Our body has its own 
internal 24-hour clock that 
guides when we sleep, 
wake up, feel hungry, stay 
active, and even how our 
mind works. This natural 
cycle is called the circadian 
rhythm. Everyone’s rhythm 
is slightly different and 
changes with age, but its 
purpose is the same — to 
keep the body working 
smoothly.
	
The circadian rhythm 
influences many functions, 
such 
as 
sleep, 
body 
temperature, 
immunity, 
hormones, 
digestion, 
memory, and how we react 
to stress. When this rhythm is disturbed, we may face problems like 
poor sleep, tiredness, mood swings, digestion issues, or difficulty in 
focusing.
	
Building habits becomes easier when we follow this natural 
rhythm. Some simple ways to align with it include:
•	
Sleeping and waking up at the same time every day
•	
Studying or working when the mind feels most alert
•	
Exercising during high-energy periods
•	
Eating meals at regular times
	
When we live in tune with our circadian rhythm, our body and 
mind function at their best. Ayurveda also teaches us that aligning 
our daily routine with nature’s cycle — sunrise, sunset, and seasonal 
changes — supports digestion, immunity, and overall balance.

Ajapā japa
Japa is the continuous repetition of a mantra which comes from the 
mouth and Ajapa comes from the breath and the heart. Japa is the 
preliminary practice of mantra repetition and ajapa is the perfection 
of this practice. It relaxes the body and mind.
Stage 1 (Preparation): Sit in a comfortable meditatative Āsana with 
your back upright and head straight. Place your hands on your 
knees in the jñāna or chin-mudrā. Become aware of your body and 
the meditation posture. Feel the position of your body from the top 
of the head to the toes. Feel your posture becoming steady and still. 
Develop total awareness of your body and maintain this stillness.
Stage 2 (Breath Awareness): Switch the awareness from your body to 
your breath. Practice slow and deep breathing. Breathe in, counting 
to five, and then breathe out, counting to five again. Concentrate on 
the breath in your throat and feel it moving through your throat. 
Gradually feel the stillness of your mind and body as a whole. Notice 
your breath moving from your navel to your throat. Focus your 
attention on your navel. Inhale slowly in a straight line from your 
navel up to your throat. Then exhale slowly in a straight line from 
your throat down to your navel. Count to five while breathing in and 
while breathing out. Continue until your breath moves along this 
fixed path without conscious effort. Watch your breath carefully, 
without missing a single breath.
Stage 3 (Frontal Passage Visualisation): Visualise the psychic passage 
between your navel and your throat as a long, thin, and transparent 
tube. It is hollow inside and open at both ends, like a flute. Move your 
awareness up and down along the outside surface. Now, visualise 
the tube from within. Move your attention across the passage up and 
down, and to the centre.
Stage 4 (Breath Rotation Inside the Psychic Passage): Feel your breath 
moving inside the frontal passage, between your navel and your 
throat. While inhaling, the breath ascends from your navel to your 
throat. While exhaling, it descends from your throat to your navel. 
Feel as your awareness moves up and down the centre of the psychic 
passage, along with your breath.


185
Yogamaya Jīvana
Stage 5 (Rotation of Prāṇa): Gradually become aware of the prāṇa 
(vital energy) moving together with your breath and consciousness. 
Prāṇa moves in form of energy with the breath. This flow of prāṇa 
or energy between the navel and the throat creates the psychic 
passage. Visualise the thin sparkling stream of energy flowing inside 
from inside your navel to your throat as you breathe in and from 
your throat to your navel as you breathe out. With practice you will 
spontaneously feel a powerful current of energy flowing up and 
down the psychic passage.
Stage 6 (Rotation of So’ham): Leave the awareness of the prāṇa. 
Carefully listen to the subtle sound of your breath moving up and 
down the passage. The inherent sound of your breath is Soham. As 
you breathe in here the sound made is ‘so-o-o-o-o’. As you breathe 
out, hear the sound ‘ham-m-m-m-m-m’. Feel the vibration of the 
mantra moving with the breath.
Stage 7 (Ending the Practice): Get ready to end the practice. Withdraw 
your awareness from your frontal passage and the mantra. Change 
over to normal breathing. Become aware of your body and the 
meditation posture. Feel your body from head to toe. Breathe in 
deeply and chant ‘Om’ three times.
Antar Mauna
Antar mauna, translates to inner silence. It cultivates awareness, 
mental clarity, and mastery over the mind. It follows the principle 
of Sākṣhī-bhāva (the attitude of witnessing). It is a profound method 
of inner observation and transformation. By fostering conscious 
awareness of mental processes, it leads to inner silence that is 
natural, stable, and transformative, supporting holistic well-being 
and spiritual growth. It guides the practitioner away from external 
distractions towards their inner consciousness. Learning this 
practice correctly leads to mental stability, depth of meditation, and 
self-realisation.
Step 1 (Preparation)
Sit in a comfortable meditation posture. Keep your back upright and 
head straight. Close your eyes and become aware of the whole body. 


186
Khel Praveen
Keep observing different sounds in the surroundings, nearby and far 
away, without analysing any particular sound. Shift your awareness 
to your natural, spontaneous breathing. Maintain full awareness of 
your breath for a while.
Step 2 (Awareness of Thoughts)
Now observe the thoughts that arise in your mind. Do not stop or 
change them. Focus your full attention on your thought process. 
Become aware of every thought, like clouds passing in the sky.
Step 3 (Creation and Dissolution of Thoughts)
Select any one thought and maintain this thought for some 
time. Hold it for a few moments and then leave it. Then, 
create another thought and leave it. Hold each one for a few 
minutes and dismiss it. Take your awareness to Chidākaśha
(space in front of the closed eyes), and be a witness to any thoughts 
or visions that arise. Do this for some time.
Step 4 (Experience of Thoughtlessness)
Now focus on the space between thoughts. Do not put any effort. 
Simply experience the silence. This state comes spontaneously, not 
by force. Continue doing this for some time.
Step 5 (Conclusion)
Gradually bring your awareness back to your breath. Become aware 
of your body. Rub your palms together and place them over your 
eyes. Open your eyes.

Preparing the Body and Mind for Śhreyas through Yoga Practice
One of the main obstacles that pulls us repeatedly towards Preyas 
instead of Śhreyas is laziness and a lack of routine. When the body is 
not active and there are too many thoughts in the mind, it becomes 
difficult to make wise choices, even when we know what is right.
	
Yoga teaches us that self-mastery begins with the body. By gently 
training the body through regular āsana practice, and calming the 
mind through prāṇāyāma, we develop alertness, discipline, and inner 
motivation. This daily effort (sādhana) gradually reduces laziness, 
increases energy levels, and strengthens willpower. As regularity 
grows, choosing Śhreyas becomes natural rather than forced.
	
If you are at a point where you are having to choose between 
instant gratification and long-term happiness, take a step back. Sit 
in any meditative posture — Sukhāsana, Vajrāsana or Padmāsana 
— and practice slow, deep breaths, bringing all your focus to your 
abdominal breathing till the mind settles down. You will then be in a 
clear, calm state of mind to make a wise decision.

Think of an Indian Olympian who is one of India’s youngest shooting

Here’s a secret about overcoming distractions that many students miss!
Distractions are more tempting when your goal is small and your 
mind is not focused or calm.
Higher Goals vs Smaller Goals 
“Nālpe sukham asti, bhūmaiva sukham.” (Chhāndogya-Upaniṣhad)
Meaning: “There is no joy in the small/limited. True joy is only in the 
infinite/vast.”
	
Do you know about the teacher who refused to be miserable?
	
In ancient India, the great teacher Chāṇakya went to the court of 
King Dhanānanda to warn him about a Greek invasion.
	
Instead of listening, the arrogant King insulted Chāṇakya. He 
mocked his appearance, pulled him by his hair, and threw him out 
of the palace. 
	
Now imagine this moment, a respected teacher has been 
humiliated in front of hundreds of people.
	
The Small Goal Reaction: If Chāṇakya’s goal was just “I want 
respect” or “I want a job in the palace,” this moment would have 


193
Yoga for Personal Excellence
destroyed him. He would have gone home, cried, and lived in misery 
forever. He would have been broken by shame.
	
The Big Goal Response: But Chāṇakya had a higher goal. His vision 
was to save Bharat from Greek invasion and our people from slavery.
	
Because his goal was big, the personal insult didn’t break him. It 
fueled him. He untied his hair and vowed, “I will not tie my hair until 
I replace this corrupt King with a leader who can protect the nation.” 
	
He didn’t go home to cry. He went to the streets, found a boy 
named Chandragupta, trained him, and built the Mauryan Empire. 
Chāṇakya showed us that when your eyes are fixed on a mountain 
peak, you do not care if you trip on a stone.
	
Shifting focus from a smaller goal to a higher goal creates a 
psychological shield that we can apply to our own modern lives. When 
you are in a fragile state because of a small goal— like wanting to be 
popular in school — the results are often devastating. One rude comment 
from a friend makes you sad for a week because you have nothing 
else to look forward to. Conversely, when you enter an unbreakable 
state driven by a big goal, such as wanting to become a scientist to 
solve climate change, the result changes entirely. If someone makes 
fun of your glasses or your grades, it does not matter; you are too busy 
building the future, and the insult simply bounces of you.
	
The same logic applies to our daily habits and how we sustain 
our motivation over time.
	
A narrow or smaller goal might be to be fit to look good, which 
makes it very easy to quit when things get tough. However, a higher goal 
means you want to be fit so you have the strength to help others and 
serve your country, a mindset that makes it almost impossible to quit.

and helps your group stay peaceful so everyone can do well together.
Action Plan
The 3-Breath Rule: Before reacting to anger, take 3 deep breaths.
Empathy: Ask, “How can I support my friend right now?” instead of 
judging them.
Daily Practices that Support Emotional Control
•	
Nāḍī-śhodhana Prāṇāyāma: 9 rounds
•	
Bhrāmarī Prāṇāyāma: 5 times
•	
Slow, Deep Breathing: 20 times
•	
Short Periods of Dhyāna: 5 minutes
Step 3: Leadership and Responsibility (Sevā)
Leadership is not about being the boss. It is about sevā, which means 
service.
The Trap: “I won’t volunteer because it’s too much work” or “What 
if others laugh at me?”
The Yogic Solution: Karma-Yoga is about selfless action. It teaches 
you to work for the satisfaction of self and not reward. Do things 
because you feel they are right, like taking care of your surroundings. 
If the classroom is messy, a leader cleans it, not for praise, but because 
it is the right thing to do. When you focus on helping others, you stop 
worrying about what people think. People follow a person who is 
genuine, works selflessly and cares for others. They are true leaders.
Action Plan
Take Initiative: Do not wait to be asked. If you see clutter, pick it up.
Help others shine: Teach a concept you know to a friend who is 
struggling with it.
Step Out: Volunteer for community service or school responsibilities.
Step 4: Self-Confidence (The Prāṇa Connection)
As a student, it is easy to lose confidence because of social media or 
comparing yourself to others.


200
Khel Praveen
The Trap: “I am not good enough because I do not look like that 
influencer.”
The Higher Goal Solution: Confidence is not about how you look 
but about how useful you are. When you know you are helping your 
family, friends or community, you feel engaged and important. You 
stop worrying about your appearance and focus on what you can do 
for others.
The Yogic Solution: Yoga builds Prāṇa, or energy. When you stand 
tall in a yoga pose, you are not just stretching, you are also learning 
to stand strong against peer pressure.
Action Plan
Practice Āsanas: Vīrabhadrāsana (Warrior Pose): This helps you 
build the strength and courage of a warrior who stands up for what 
is right.
Vṛikṣhāsana (Tree Pose): This pose helps you find balance, even 
when life feels chaotic. Padmāsana (Lotus Pose): Use this pose to sit 
quietly and meditate, thinking about your goals.
Sevā: When you feel low, go help someone. Doing a good deed is the 
fastest way to boost confidence.
Step 5: Self-Awareness and Continuous Growth (Svādhyāya)
The Trap: “I know enough, I do not need to learn more.”
The Higher Goal Solution: The world faces big problems like 
climate change, pollution, and poverty. To solve these complex 
issues, innovation, research and knowledge of numerous subjects is 
necessary. To be happy and uplift the needy, continuous learning and 
action is required. You can help the planet with your small actions. 
The Yogic Solution: Svādhyāya means self-study. Think about your 
actions. Are you becoming someone who helps the world, or just 
someone who takes from it?
Action Plan
Seek Feedback: Ask, “How can I improve to be a better team player?”
Daily Reflection: “Did I help someone today? Did I waste my potential 
today?”

Yoga for Personal Excellence
Part 2: The Sunday Reflection
At the end of the week, answer these three questions to practice self-
awareness:
The Win: What was one moment this week where I felt truly proud 
of my behaviour?
The Challenge: Which distraction was the hardest to fight this week? 
How will I handle it better next week?
The Higher Goal: How did my actions this week help someone else 
or contribute to my home or school environment?
Conclusion: The Ant and the Vulture
Excellence is not a quick race, but a long journey. You might feel small 
right now. But if you keep moving forward with a clear purpose to 
improve yourself and help others, you will go farther than you think. 
	
To support this journey towards personal excellence, a unique 
yoga module has been designed, requiring a few minutes of daily 
practice. When sincerely and consistently followed, these qualities not 
only reflect on the yoga mat but also lead to observable improvements 
in physical vitality, mental clarity, confidence, discipline, and the 
ability to make calm, wise choices in challenging situations.
